cards.definition: the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms
a cell with a true nucleus enclosed by a membrane — includes all plant and animal cells
a cell with no true nucleus — DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm. Bacteria are prokaryotes
a thin, flexible layer surrounding all cells — controls what enters and leaves
a rigid outer layer made of cellulose surrounding plant cells — provides support and shape
organelles where aerobic respiration occurs — produce ATP (energy) for the cell
organelle in plant cells containing chlorophyll — the site of photosynthesis
how many times larger an image appears compared to the actual object (Magnification = Image size ÷ Actual size)
a microscope using visible light and lenses — can magnify up to about ×1500 and shows living specimens
a microscope using electrons instead of light — far higher magnification and resolution than a light microscope, but cannot show living specimens
a biological catalyst — speeds up chemical reactions in living organisms without being used up
the specific region of an enzyme where a substrate binds — has a complementary shape to the substrate
the idea that an enzyme's active site is a specific shape that only fits one substrate — like a key fits only one lock
when an enzyme's active site changes shape permanently due to high temperature or extreme pH — the enzyme can no longer function
the conditions at which an enzyme works most efficiently — different enzymes have different optima
a biological molecule made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen — used for energy (glucose) and structure (cellulose)
a biological molecule made of amino acids — used for enzymes, antibodies, hormones, and structural components
a biological molecule including fats and oils — used for energy storage, cell membranes, and insulation
deoxyribonucleic acid — the molecule that carries genetic information in cells, made of two strands in a double helix
the movement of molecules from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration — a passive process requiring no energy
cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells — used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction
cell division producing four genetically unique cells with half the chromosome number — used to make gametes (sex cells)
a thread-like structure of DNA carrying genetic information — human cells have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs
an undifferentiated cell that can divide and develop into different cell types
the process by which a cell becomes specialised for a particular function — e.g. a stem cell becoming a nerve cell
a specialised nerve cell that transmits electrical impulses around the body
the gap between two neurones — impulses cross the gap using chemical signals called neurotransmitters
the pathway of an automatic response: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
the brain and spinal cord — processes information and coordinates responses
a cell or organ that detects stimuli — e.g. the retina detects light; taste buds detect chemicals
a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein — determines an organism's characteristics
a version of a gene — most genes have two or more alleles (e.g. dominant and recessive)
an allele whose characteristic is always expressed, even if only one copy is present
an allele whose characteristic is only expressed when two copies are present (homozygous recessive)
having two identical alleles for a gene — e.g. BB or bb
having two different alleles for a gene — e.g. Bb
the genetic make-up of an organism — the alleles it carries (e.g. Bb)
the physical characteristics of an organism produced by its genotype and environment
a grid used to predict the outcomes and probabilities of a genetic cross
a random change in the DNA sequence — can be harmful, beneficial, or neutral
a microorganism that causes disease — includes bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists
a disease that can be passed from one organism to another — e.g. influenza, malaria, tuberculosis
a disease that cannot be passed between organisms — e.g. cancer, diabetes, heart disease
the body's defence against pathogens — includes white blood cells (phagocytes and lymphocytes)
the process by which white blood cells (phagocytes) engulf and destroy pathogens
a protein produced by lymphocytes that binds to and destroys a specific pathogen
a protein on the surface of a pathogen that triggers an immune response
introducing a harmless form of a pathogen into the body to stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies without causing disease
a medicine that kills or inhibits the growth of bacteria — does not work against viruses
when bacteria evolve to no longer be killed by an antibiotic — caused by overuse and misuse of antibiotics
